content.text: Why Fine-Tune Embeddings for RAG

General-purpose embedding models (text-embedding-3-small, bge-large-en) are trained on broad web corpora. They perform well on common domains but struggle when the target corpus uses specialized vocabulary — medical terminology, legal citations, proprietary product names, or domain-specific abbreviations. Fine-tuning adapts the embedding space so that domain-specific query-passage pairs are pulled closer together. The result is higher retrieval precision and recall for domain queries without changing the base model's general capabilities.

attrs.title: Domain corpus → LLM-generated pairs → contrastive training → evaluate → deploy

content.text: Generating Training Pairs with an LLM

Embedding fine-tuning requires (query, positive_passage) pairs — examples where the query should retrieve that passage. Collecting these from real user logs is ideal but slow. An LLM can generate synthetic queries for each passage, producing thousands of training pairs from your document corpus in an afternoon. LlamaIndex provides a utility that automates this.

# pip install llama-index llama-index-embeddings-openai
from llama_index.finetuning import generate_qa_embedding_pairs
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI

# Load domain documents
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()

# Generate (query, passage) pairs — ~2–5 queries per document node
qa_dataset = generate_qa_embedding_pairs(
    nodes=documents,   # SimpleDirectoryReader outputs are Node objects
    llm=OpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"),
    num_questions_per_chunk=3,
    output_path="train_pairs.json",
)

print(f"Generated {len(qa_dataset.queries)} training pairs")
# Example output: 1,200 pairs from 400 document nodes

# Inspect a sample pair
qid = list(qa_dataset.queries.keys())[0]
print("Query:", qa_dataset.queries[qid])
print("Passage ID:", qa_dataset.relevant_docs[qid][0])
Fine-Tuning with sentence-transformers

The sentence-transformers library provides a high-level API for fine-tuning bi-encoder embedding models with contrastive loss (MultipleNegativesRankingLoss). This loss treats every other passage in the batch as a hard negative, which forces the model to distinguish the correct passage from plausible but incorrect ones.

# pip install sentence-transformers datasets
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
import json

# Load generated pairs
with open("train_pairs.json") as f:
    pairs_data = json.load(f)

# Build InputExample list — MultipleNegativesRankingLoss only needs positive pairs
train_examples = [
    InputExample(texts=[item["query"], item["passage"]])
    for item in pairs_data["pairs"]
]

# Base model — start from a strong general-purpose bi-encoder
model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")

train_dataloader = DataLoader(train_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=32)
# MultipleNegativesRankingLoss: other passages in batch become in-batch negatives
train_loss = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model)

model.fit(
    train_objectives=[(train_dataloader, train_loss)],
    epochs=3,
    warmup_steps=100,
    output_path="./finetuned_embedding_model",
    show_progress_bar=True,
    evaluation_steps=200,
)

print("Fine-tuned model saved to ./finetuned_embedding_model")
Using a Fine-Tuned Model in LangChain

Swapping the embedding model in a LangChain RAG pipeline requires only one change — the embedding class used to build the vector store. The fine-tuned model is loaded with HuggingFaceEmbeddings pointing at the saved directory.

from langchain_community.embeddings import HuggingFaceEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_core.documents import Document

# Load fine-tuned model from saved directory
finetuned_embeddings = HuggingFaceEmbeddings(
    model_name="./finetuned_embedding_model",
    model_kwargs={"device": "cpu"},   # "cuda" if GPU available
    encode_kwargs={"normalize_embeddings": True},  # Required for cosine similarity
)

# Rebuild vector store with fine-tuned embeddings
# (must re-embed all documents — old index used different embedding space)
docs = [Document(page_content="Electronics: 30-day return window.")]
vector_store = FAISS.from_documents(docs, finetuned_embeddings)
retriever = vector_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 5})

results = retriever.invoke("What is the return policy for electronics?")
print(results[0].page_content)

# Compare against base model
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
base_embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
base_store = FAISS.from_documents(docs, base_embeddings)
base_retriever = base_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 5})
base_results = base_retriever.invoke("What is the return policy for electronics?")
# Run eval metrics on both to quantify the improvement
Evaluating Fine-Tuned vs Base Embeddings

Always measure the improvement before deploying a fine-tuned model. Use the same evaluation set and compute NDCG@10 and Recall@10 for both the base and fine-tuned models. A fine-tuned model that doesn't improve by at least 5 percentage points is unlikely to justify the maintenance overhead.

from llama_index.finetuning import EmbeddingQAFinetuneDataset
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding
from llama_index.embeddings.huggingface import HuggingFaceEmbedding
from llama_index.finetuning.embeddings.common import evaluate_embeddings

# Load held-out evaluation set (separate from training set)
val_dataset = EmbeddingQAFinetuneDataset.from_json("val_pairs.json")

base_embed  = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")
tuned_embed = HuggingFaceEmbedding(model_name="./finetuned_embedding_model")

# Returns a DataFrame with hit_rate and MRR per query
base_results  = evaluate_embeddings(val_dataset, embed_model=base_embed)
tuned_results = evaluate_embeddings(val_dataset, embed_model=tuned_embed)

print(f"Base  — Hit Rate: {base_results['hit_rate'].mean():.3f}, "
      f"MRR: {base_results['mrr'].mean():.3f}")
print(f"Tuned — Hit Rate: {tuned_results['hit_rate'].mean():.3f}, "
      f"MRR: {tuned_results['mrr'].mean():.3f}")
# Example output:
# Base  — Hit Rate: 0.623, MRR: 0.501
# Tuned — Hit Rate: 0.821, MRR: 0.712  (+20pp hit rate improvement)
When Not to Fine-Tune
Fine-tuning is not always the right answer. Consider these alternatives first.
Try a stronger base model first:
text-embedding-3-large or bge-large-en-v1.5 often closes the gap without any fine-tuning, at lower cost than a training run.
Fix chunking before fine-tuning:
Poor precision is often caused by oversized chunks diluting embeddings. Re-chunking to 256 tokens can improve Precision@5 by 10–15 pp without any model training.
Add a reranker instead:
A cross-encoder reranker over the top-20 results costs less to deploy than fine-tuning a bi-encoder and often provides comparable quality improvements.
Fine-tune only when you have ≥5,000 pairs:
Models trained on fewer pairs frequently overfit and perform worse than the base model on edge cases not represented in the training set.
In-Batch Negatives and Contrastive Loss

MultipleNegativesRankingLoss treats every other question in the batch as a negative example for a given (query, passage) pair. This is highly sample-efficient — a batch of 32 pairs yields 31 implicit negatives per anchor. The loss pushes the query embedding toward its positive passage and away from all other passages in the batch. Hard negatives (passages that are topically close but factually different) dramatically improve discrimination; mine them using a fast ANN index on your base model before fine-tuning.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample
from sentence_transformers.losses import MultipleNegativesRankingLoss
from sentence_transformers.evaluation import InformationRetrievalEvaluator
import faiss, numpy as np

def mine_hard_negatives(corpus: list[str], queries: list[str],
                         model: SentenceTransformer, top_k: int = 5) -> list[InputExample]:
    """For each query, retrieve top-k passages; use rank 2–5 as hard negatives."""
    c_emb = model.encode(corpus, batch_size=64, normalize_embeddings=True)
    q_emb = model.encode(queries, batch_size=64, normalize_embeddings=True)
    index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(c_emb.shape[1])
    index.add(c_emb)
    _, I = index.search(q_emb, top_k + 1)

    examples = []
    for qi, (query, idxs) in enumerate(zip(queries, I)):
        positive = corpus[idxs[0]]           # rank 1 = positive
        for neg_idx in idxs[1:top_k]:         # ranks 2–5 = hard negatives
            examples.append(InputExample(texts=[query, positive, corpus[neg_idx]]))
    return examples

# Evaluator on held-out split (20% of pairs)
evaluator = InformationRetrievalEvaluator(
    queries=val_queries,           # {qid: query_text}
    corpus=val_corpus,             # {cid: passage_text}
    relevant_docs=val_relevant,    # {qid: set(cid)}
    name="val_retrieval",
)
Asymmetric vs Symmetric Encoding

Symmetric models assume query and passage come from the same distribution (e.g. sentence similarity). Retrieval is inherently asymmetric — queries are short and conversational while passages are dense and factual. BGE and E5 models ship separate query/passage prefixes ("query: ..." / "passage: ...") to handle this. Preserve this asymmetry in your fine-tuning pairs: the first element of every InputExample is always the query, the second always the passage. Mixing them produces embeddings that perform well in evaluation but poorly at retrieval time.
Hyperparameter Guide for Embedding Fine-Tuning

Embedding fine-tuning is sensitive to batch size (larger = more in-batch negatives = better signal), learning rate (too high destroys pre-trained alignment), and warmup (prevents early collapse). The table below provides a starting point; adjust based on your validation MRR curve.
Hyperparameter
Recommended
Notes
Batch size
Larger = more negatives; GPU memory bound
Learning rate
Start low; higher rates cause catastrophic forgetting
Warmup steps
10% of total steps
Prevents early gradient explosions
Epochs
Use early stopping on val MRR; avoid overfitting
Training pairs
≥ 5,000 recommended
< 1,000 rarely generalizes; use stronger base model instead
Production Deployment and Model Versioning

After fine-tuning, publish the model to a local registry or Hugging Face Hub and rebuild your vector index using the new embeddings. Old index files are incompatible with new model weights — re-embedding the entire corpus is mandatory. Version the model and index together (e.g., v1.2.0 maps to both model and index), and keep the previous version warm for instant rollback. Monitor embedding distribution shift in production by periodically measuring cosine similarity between live query embeddings and your validation query centroid.

import os
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_community.embeddings import HuggingFaceEmbeddings

MODEL_VERSION = "v1.2.0"
MODEL_PATH = f"./models/embedding_{MODEL_VERSION}"
INDEX_PATH = f"./indexes/faiss_{MODEL_VERSION}"

def promote_model(trainer_output_dir: str, version: str):
    """Save fine-tuned model and rebuild the FAISS index."""
    model = SentenceTransformer(trainer_output_dir)
    model.save(f"./models/embedding_{version}")
    print(f"Model saved to ./models/embedding_{version}")

def rebuild_index(docs: list[str], version: str):
    """Re-embed all passages with the new model version."""
    embeddings = HuggingFaceEmbeddings(
        model_name=f"./models/embedding_{version}",
        model_kwargs={"device": "cpu"},
        encode_kwargs={"normalize_embeddings": True},
    )
    db = FAISS.from_texts(docs, embeddings)
    db.save_local(f"./indexes/faiss_{version}")
    print(f"Index rebuilt at ./indexes/faiss_{version} ({len(docs)} passages)")

def monitor_embedding_drift(model: SentenceTransformer,
                             live_queries: list[str],
                             baseline_centroid: list[float]) -> float:
    """Returns cosine similarity between live query mean and baseline."""
    import numpy as np
    live_emb = model.encode(live_queries, normalize_embeddings=True)
    live_centroid = live_emb.mean(axis=0)
    baseline = np.array(baseline_centroid)
    return float(np.dot(live_centroid, baseline))  # high = stable distribution
Summary
Fine-tuning adapts embedding space to domain vocabulary and query patterns, improving retrieval precision and recall by 15–40% on specialized corpora.
Use an LLM to generate synthetic (query, passage) training pairs from your corpus — llama_index.finetuning.generate_qa_embedding_pairs automates this.
sentence-transformers with MultipleNegativesRankingLoss is the standard training setup; it uses in-batch negatives without requiring hand-labeled negatives.
Swapping to a fine-tuned model in LangChain requires rebuilding the vector store — the embedding spaces are incompatible.
Always benchmark fine-tuned vs base on a held-out eval set; if improvement is under 5 pp, try a stronger base model or reranker instead.
